title: Your First Workout: Understanding Fitness Through a Basic Circuit
description: Experience the science of fitness training through a simple body-weight circuit that illustrates cardiovascular response, muscular endurance, and the FITT principle. No equipment needed.

steps.instruction: Before exercising, read about fundamental movement skills and fitness training. Then check your resting heart rate: place two fingers on your wrist pulse and count beats for 15 seconds, multiply by 4. Record this number. This is your resting heart rate baseline.
steps.expectedOutcome: You have your resting heart rate baseline and understand that it measures cardiovascular efficiency
steps.hint: The average adult resting heart rate is 60-100 BPM. Athletes often have 40-60 BPM due to higher stroke volume from training.

steps.instruction: 5-minute dynamic warm-up (no static stretching yet -- read why!): 30 seconds each of arm circles, leg swings, hip circles, high knees, jumping jacks, and inchworms. Your heart rate should rise. Read about injury prevention to understand why dynamic warm-up precedes activity.
steps.expectedOutcome: You understand why dynamic warm-up is more effective than static stretching pre-activity for injury prevention and performance
steps.hint: Notice how each exercise activates muscles you will use in the circuit -- this is the 'sport-specific' principle of warm-up

steps.instruction: Circuit (2 rounds): 10 squats, 8 push-ups (modify as needed), 20 jumping jacks, 10-second plank, 15-second wall sit. Rest 30 seconds between exercises. Check heart rate immediately after the last exercise in round 1. Read about FITT principle.
steps.expectedOutcome: You experience the cardiovascular demand of circuit training and understand how Frequency, Intensity, Time, and Type interact
steps.hint: If the circuit is too easy (heart rate below 130), reduce rest time. If overwhelming, extend rest to 60 seconds -- this is the FITT principle in action.

steps.instruction: 5-minute cool-down: walk slowly, then read about flexibility. Hold each stretch 30 seconds: quad stretch, hamstring stretch, chest stretch, shoulder cross-body. Notice how warm muscles allow greater range of motion.
steps.expectedOutcome: You understand why static stretching is effective post-workout and how temperature affects muscle extensibility
steps.hint: This is the right time for static stretching -- muscles are warm and the nervous system is less guarded against lengthening

steps.instruction: 5 minutes after the cool-down, check your heart rate again. How quickly did it return toward resting? Read about fitness training -- faster recovery is a marker of cardiovascular fitness that improves with training.
steps.expectedOutcome: You can compare your exercise and recovery heart rates and understand heart rate recovery as a fitness indicator
